# How can you help me?

I have three asks for the survival-analysis people in the room.
They run in both directions, but today I am mostly asking.

## Ask 1: review my hazard-based delay estimator

I have an open PR in `primarycensored` that adds non-parametric, hazard-based delay
estimation alongside the existing parametric families.

- It estimates the delay PMF on fixed bins either directly (a simplex) or through
  per-bin discrete-time hazards, with a smooth random-walk or IID random-effects prior
  on `logit(hazard)`.
- Hazards and PMF are interconvertible (`hazards_to_pmf()`), and both fit through the
  same doubly-censored, right-truncated likelihood as the parametric distributions.
- The point is to drop the parametric-shape assumption while keeping the censoring and
  truncation handling honest.

::: {.callout-note}
## The ask
[primarycensored#312](https://github.com/epinowcast/primarycensored/pull/312):
is the discrete-time hazard formulation right?
What should I be comparing against: non-parametric MLE, Turnbull's estimator,
hazard-based interval-censored likelihoods?
Where would a survival analyst say I have reinvented something, badly?
:::

## Ask 2: pairwise / contact-interval survival models

I want to push Eben Kenah's pairwise survival idea further and connect it to the
event-time view of serial and generation intervals.

- [Kenah (2011)](https://doi.org/10.1093/biostatistics/kxq068) treats the contact
  interval as a time-to-event variable and does survival analysis on who-infects-whom.
- That feels like the right language for the dependent point process behind generation
  and serial intervals, not iid survival times.
- The transmission data are interval-censored on both ends, and the "at-risk" set
  changes as susceptibles are depleted.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The ask
How do we bring contact-interval survival machinery to dependent transmission data?
Where does it break when start times are unobserved, events are interval-censored, and
the risk set is itself a function of the process we are estimating?
:::

## Ask 3: why are you not anxious about double interval censoring?

An honest question. We spend a lot of effort on primary plus secondary interval
censoring, dynamical bias, and real-time truncation. You handle interval censoring
routinely and seem far less worried.

- You have Turnbull, interval-censored likelihoods, left and right truncation as
  standard tools.
- So: what are we over-engineering, and what is genuinely different on our side?
- My current guesses: the **start time is itself censored**, not a clean enrolment date;
  the events are **dependent through transmission**; and we estimate in **real time**, so
  the data are right-truncated as they arrive.
- Tell me which of these is real and which is us reinventing your wheels.
